coredata.dc:title: Constructing dense and hydrophilic forward osmosis membrane by cross-linking reaction of graphene quantum dots with monomers for enhanced selectivity and stability
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

Li, Peng
Li, Nan
Wang, Wei
Guo, Changsheng
Shan, Mingjing
Qian, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: This paper reports a novel thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane with a dense, flat, and hydrophilic polyamide (PA) layer. The atypical PA structure was obtained by the cross-linking reaction of graphene oxide quantum dots containing amino groups (NH2-GOQDs) with triacyl chloride and polyamide oligomers. And the resulting TFN membrane showed a flat (small-scale ridge structure) and smooth surface. Meanwhile, the introduction of oxygen-containing and amino functional groups increased surface hydrophilicity. The reaction of amino groups on the NH2-GOQDs with acid chloride groups and the carboxyl groups (in the linear part of the polyamide) enhanced the degree of cross-linking of the PA layer, forming a compact surface. Owning to the dense surface structure, excellent hydrophilicity, and small water transmission distance, the optimized TFN membrane exhibited an enhanced water flux of 26.57 L⋅m−2⋅h−1 with a low reverse salt flux of 6.0 g⋅m−2⋅h−1. Furthermore, nano-indentation/scratch results showed the interface adhesion between substrate and PA layer was improved due to the physical anchoring of NH2-GOQDs in the substrate. And in the long-term FO test, the TFN membrane showed stable selectivity. This work proves that the targeted structural design of the PA layer at the nanoscale will have a positive impact on desalination field.

head.ce:title: Constructing dense and hydrophilic forward osmosis membrane by cross-linking reaction of graphene quantum dots with monomers for enhanced selectivity and stability

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Textiles Science and Engineering, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes
School of Textiles Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Textiles Science and Engineering, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper reports a novel thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane with a dense, flat, and hydrophilic polyamide (PA) layer. The atypical PA structure was obtained by the cross-linking reaction of graphene oxide quantum dots containing amino groups (NH2-GOQDs) with triacyl chloride and polyamide oligomers. And the resulting TFN membrane showed a flat (small-scale ridge structure) and smooth surface. Meanwhile, the introduction of oxygen-containing and amino functional groups increased surface hydrophilicity. The reaction of amino groups on the NH2-GOQDs with acid chloride groups and the carboxyl groups (in the linear part of the polyamide) enhanced the degree of cross-linking of the PA layer, forming a compact surface. Owning to the dense surface structure, excellent hydrophilicity, and small water transmission distance, the optimized TFN membrane exhibited an enhanced water flux of 26.57 L⋅m−2⋅h−1 with a low reverse salt flux of 6.0 g⋅m−2⋅h−1. Furthermore, nano-indentation/scratch results showed the interface adhesion between substrate and PA layer was improved due to the physical anchoring of NH2-GOQDs in the substrate. And in the long-term FO test, the TFN membrane showed stable selectivity. This work proves that the targeted structural design of the PA layer at the nanoscale will have a positive impact on desalination field.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Graphene oxide quantum dots containing amino

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, with the acceleration of global industrialization and the increase of population, industrial wastewater rises sharply and fresh water used for drinking is in short supply and deterioration. The global water demand trend has a rise of about 1% every year, and the growth rate will continue to increase in the next 20 years [1]. Fresh water only accounts for 2.5% of the total global water resources, whereas the rest is saline [2]. In order to satisfy people's demand for potable water, extensive researches on the treatment and purification of water resources have been done. Among them, forward osmosis (FO) technology is a promising new-type membrane separation technology. In the FO process, the osmotic pressure difference on both sides of the membrane provides the driving force to spontaneously extract water in the feed solution into the draw solution. With no external pressure applied, it is not easy for pollutants to form the cake layer on the membrane surface. Therefore, the low energy consumption and good anti-fouling property promote the forward osmosis technology widely used in petrochemical [3], liquid food processing [4], wastewater treatment [5] and other fields.

As the most critical factor in FO separation technology, the FO membrane has a similar structure with reverse osmosis membrane, which consists of a porous support layer and a thin active layer (i.e. substrate and polyamide layer). At present, the support layer used for FO is mainly based on the ultrafiltration membrane obtained by phase inversion method, and the active layer is usually prepared on the substrate surface by interfacial polymerization (IP) reaction. Remarkably, the performance of FO membrane does not reach the ideal value. The driving force generated by difference of osmotic pressure decreases continuously due to the reverse diffusion of solute in the FO process, resulting in a reduction in the productivity of water. In addition, the traditional permeability–selectivity trade-off effect and internal concentration polarization (ICP) also significantly weaken the desalination efficiency of the FO membrane [6,7].

In recent studies, nanoparticles have been widely used as additives to remold the properties of the membrane itself. And these nanomaterials including oxides (e.g. SiO2 [8], TiO2 [9], and graphene oxide (GO) [10–12]), carbon nanotube [13,14], mental organic frameworks (MOFs) [15] endow the membrane surface with good hydrophilicity and improved separation performance. As one representative, GO with abundant oxygen-containing functional groups and flexibility in chemical functionalization is widely used for the construction of nanochannels within active layer. Shen et al.[16] studied the effect of GO loading on FO performance and confirmed that GO nanoparticles boosted water permeation and reduced reverse salt flux. Nevertheless, some research found that the lateral size of GO significantly affected its distribution in the polyamide (PA) layer and the degree of improvement of membrane separation performance [17,18]. Compared to GO with large lateral dimension, small GO flakes provided shorter transmission paths for water molecules. Graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs), as a novel carbon nanomaterial, has single-layer or few-layer graphene structure with the lateral size of only several nanometers [19]. GOQDs can effectively improve the surface hydrophilicity of membrane and reduce the thickness of active layer. Zhao et al. [20] coated GOQDs on the surface of polyamide nanofiltration membrane by pressure-assisted filtration. The results showed an improved hydrophilic surface and stronger electronegativity. At the pressure of 100 psi, the water flux increased by 15 LMH and higher salt retention was maintained. S. Fatemeh Seyedpour et al. [21] embedded GOQDs into the PA layer of thin-film composite (TFC) membrane, and the novel thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane showed excellent FO performance and anti-fouling property. However, the physical blending of nonreactive additive in the polymer matrix has little effect on the chain structure [22] and the density of the membrane surface, even deteriorates interfacial adhesion force between substrate and PA layer [23,24]. Furthermore, the nanoparticles will gradually be washed away in the cleaning process, so the degree of improvement for permeation and separation performance by this method is limited to some extent.

In order to solve the above problems, many scholars have conducted designs on the polyamide structure of active layer. As is known to all, the traditional PA layer is built by the polymerization reaction of ammonia monomer and acid chloride monomer at the interface between organic phase and water phase. The content of amino groups or acyl chloride groups can greatly affect the degree of IP reaction. In order to increase the permeability of water during FO process, Jiang et al.[25] prepared the active layer with a low degree of cross-linking, which was obtained by dopamine (DA) as ammonia monomer. Compared with the traditional TFC membrane, the DA/TMC composite membrane exhibited an improved water flux, but the salt interception was poor. In order to avoid compromising salt rejection, Xu et al. [26] mixed DA into the MPD aqueous solution and prepared a novel FO membrane with a proper cross-linking degree by adjusting the mass ratio of DA and MPD. Under the optimal mass ratio of DA/MPD, the FO membrane exhibited excellent water flux and comparable salt retention. Although decreasing the cross-linking degree can improve the water flux, this method is often accompanied by high salt flux. Xu et al. [27] studied the influence of monomer concentration on the physical and chemical structure of polyamide in a wide range. The experimental results showed that degree of cross-linking of the PA layer increased with the increase of ammonia monomer concentration, which led to the rise of salt rejection rate. Interestingly, wang et al. [28] found that polydopamine particles with small size were beneficial to increase the cross-linking degree of PA layer, thus improving the membrane selectivity. Therefore, in order to break the traditional trade-off effect (i.e. increasing water flux and reducing salt flux), the hydrophilic nanoparticle as reactive monomer which contains active functional groups is expected to chemically cross-link with PA molecular chain and then improve the FO performance.

In this study, we prepared graphene oxide quantum dots containing amino groups (NH2-GOQDs) with ammonia and citric acid through the hydrothermal process (as shown in Fig. S1). Then NH2-GOQDs were introduced to the MPD aqueous solution as reactant to participate in IP reaction. The abundant amino groups could chemically cross-link with acid chloride groups and carboxyl groups, thereby forming a dense, flat and defect-free PA layer. Furthermore, the chemical reaction promoted the connection between the substrate and the PA layer, and improved the stability of the nanoparticles in the filtration process and the durability of the PA layer. In the process of interfacial polymerization, all possible reactions are shown in Fig. 1 . In addition, considering that there are more hydrophilic functional groups at the edge of NH2-GOQDs, the driving force for water transmission across the membrane could be improved. Combined with the unique advantages of quantum dots, a novel TFN membrane with atypical PA structure by chemical cross-linking method was prepared.

Polyethersulfone (PES) obtained from Solvay S.A. was used to fabricate support layer. N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) provided by Fuyu Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin) was employed as the solvent of polymer. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) provided by Kermel Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin) was used as pore-forming agent for the membrane support layer. Citric acid (CA) was purchased from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.. Ammonia was supplied from Tianjin Jinbohua Chemical Co., Ltd. and used without further purification. M-phenylene diamine (MPD) and 1,3,5-benzenetricarbonyl trichloride (TMC, ≥98%) as active monomers for IP reaction were provided by Damao Chemical Reagent Factory and Dibo Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai). NaCl (≥99.5%), hexane (≥98%) and other chemical reagents were supplied from Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Technologies Co., Ltd. (Tianjin).

Inspired by the previous works [29,30] of citric acid pyrolysis to obtain GOQDs, 30 ml ammonia water and120 ml citric acid aqueous solution (100 mg/L) were mixed in the Teflon-lined autoclave. Then the mixture was heated at 180°C for 24 h to carry out hydrothermal reaction. The resulting solution was expected to obtain NH2-GOQDs through dialysis and freeze-drying. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, D8ADVANCE, Germany) was used to study the chemical composition of NH2-GOQDs. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, TENSOR37, Germany) was conducted to confirm the existence of amino groups and oxygen-containing groups with the wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Discover) analysis was applied to characterize the crystalline of NH2-GOQDs in the continuous scanning mode (λ = 1.54 Å). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM Hitachi H7650) was employed to observe the nanoparticle size and distribution of NH2-GOQDs. The height of nanoparticle was investigated by atomic force microscope (AFM, Icon, American).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of TFC and TFN FO membranes
ce:section.ce:para: PES ultrafiltration membrane prepared by non-solvent induced phase separation method was used as substrate. Briefly, the polymer solution containing 15 wt% PES, 0.5 wt% PVP, and 84.5 wt% DMF was magnetically stirred in a water bath at 70°C for 10 h. The obtained solution remained overnight to remove bubbles. Then adjust the scraper height of the automatic coating machine (AFA-III) to 150 μm, and pour the mixed solution evenly on the glass plate for scraping. And then the glass plate containing the solution was immersed in the DI water coagulation bath instantly to undergo the phase separation process at 25°C. The formed membrane was transferred in deionized water tank for 24 h to remove residual solvent before any further use.

The PA layer was prepared by traditional IP reaction. Firstly, the PES membrane was sandwiched between two polytetrafluoroethylene frames and keep the coating layer exposed. The aqueous solution of 2 wt% MPD added with appropriate concentration NH2-GOQDs was ultrasonicated for 30 min. Then, pour the mixed solution on the membrane for 2 min to ensure that surface of support layer can be fully wetted. After 2 min, the excess solution was removed gently using a rubber roller. Afterwards, 0.15 wt% TMC organic (hexane) solution was poured on the membrane surface containing an aqueous solution layer to cause IP reaction. Excess solution was drained after 2 min contact. The membrane was air-dried in an oven at 85°C for further IP reaction and hexane evaporation for 5 min. The as-made membrane was washed with n-hexane and stored in distilled water for testing. The synthesized composite membranes were denoted TFC, TFN-1, TFN-2, TFN-3, TFN-4, TFN-5 and the corresponding concentration of NH2-GOQDs is shown in Table 1 .

Functional groups of FO membrane were characterized by FTIR (TENSOR37, Germany). XRD (Bruker D8 Discover) was used to determine crystal structure of membrane. Chemical components and chemical bonds of membrane were assessed by XPS (D8ADVANCE, Germany). The surface roughness of FO membrane was investigated by AFM (Icon, American). Scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S4800) was used to characterize surface structure of membrane. The surface hydrophilicity of FO membrane was evaluated by contact angle goniometer (JC2000D1, China). Electrokinetic analyzer (Sur PASS 3) was used to analyze the surface zeta potential of membrane. The height of ridge on the PA layer was assessed by focused ion beam dual-beam microscopy (FIB-SEM, crossbeam 550, Germany), and the cross-section of membrane was observed with the sample table rotated by 53°. The interfacial adhesion between PA layer and substrate was probed by the in-situ nano-indentation/scratch technique (TI980, BRUKER, Germany). In short, when the probe moves on the surface of the membrane, the normal force caused by the lifting of the probe before the membrane is scratched increases with the increase of the normal depth. When the PA layer is separated from the substrate, the normal force decreases sharply and the ratio of lateral force (LF) to normal force (NF) increases. Therefore, there will be an obvious data jump point in the LF/NF-scratch time curve. The value of the normal force corresponding to this point is considered as the interfacial binding force between substrate and PA layer. And the schematic diagram of nano-indentation/scratch technique is shown in Fig. S2.

The pure water flux (A) was measured at room temperature by a RO cross-flow system with the effective area of 12.56 cm2 under 2 bar pressure. Deionized water was used as feed solution. Before testing, the prepared membrane was pre-pressed at 4 bar for 30 min to achieve stability, and then the applied pressure was adjusted to 2 bar (

A lab-scale FO filtration unit was used for evaluating permeability performance under FO mode (active layer towards feed solution) and PRO mode (active layer towards draw solution). 0.5 M NaCl aqueous solution and DI water were used as draw solution and feed solution, respectively. The specification of membrane cell was 5 cm × 6 cm and the cross-flow rate of solution was adjusted to 1.5 L/min. The electronic balance connected with the computer was used to reflect the change of feed solution weight in real time, and the computer recorded the continuous change value (test for the first 30 min was used to stabilize the filtration system). The change of the salt concentration in the feed solution which can be further used to assess reverse solute flux across FO membrane was detected using the conductivity meter. The water flux (Jv , L⋅m−2⋅h−1, abbreviated as LMH) was calculated by Eq. (5):

The chemical structures of NH2-GOQDs were investigated by XPS and FTIR. As observed in Fig. 2 a, the XPS survey spectrum shows three main peaks at 284 eV, 400 eV and 532 eV, respectively corresponding to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s signals. Furthermore, the high-resolution spectra of C 1s (Fig. S3a) presents four energy peaks located at 284.6 eV, 285.4 eV, 287.7 eV and 288.4 eV which were attributed to CC/CC in graphene, CN, CO and CO groups [31]. In addition, the deconvolution of N 1s XPS spectra is showed in Fig. S3b and the result indicated the presence of NH2 (399.6 eV) and NC (401.3 eV) [32]. FTIR spectrometry was employed to further identify the surface functional groups of NH2-GOQDs. As shown in Fig. 2b, the stretching vibration peaks at 3210 cm−1 and 3041 cm−1 could be attributed to NH bonds [33]. In addition, the absorption peaks of amide could be observed in NH2-GOQDs: the presence of absorption peak at 1659 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of CO bonds [34] (amide Ⅰ). The absorption peak near 1549 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of NH bonds (amide II). And the absorption peaks at 1394 cm−1 corresponded to the CN stretching vibration of primary amides [33] (amide III). These results show that ammonia can effectively combine with citric acid to form NH2-GOQDs under hydrothermal reaction condition. Similar to oxygen-containing functional groups, these amine functional groups were preponderantly grafted on the edge of graphene oxide layers [35]. The existence of hydrophilic groups on the surface of graphene oxide quantum dots can be conducive to its uniform dispersion in aqueous solution.

The phase structure of NH2-GOQDs was also characterized by the XRD pattern, as shown in Fig. S4. The wide peak at 2θ = 23° corresponded to the interlayer spacing about 0.38 nm according to the Bragg equation (2d sin θ = nλ). Compared with the structure of graphite (d = 0.34 nm), the introduction of amino and oxygen-containing functional groups was the main reason for the increase of d value. In addition, Fig. 2c shows the typical TEM image of the synthesized NH2-GOQDs. A good dispersibility in aqueous solution is found. Therefore, NH2-GOQDs are expected to be a satisfactory material for IP process due to the good interfacial miscibility. The TEM image also further reveals the diameter of all NH2-GOQDs is mostly less than 10 nm, and the average particle size is 4.68 nm which can be found in Fig. 2d. Furthermore, the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 2e) shows a clear in-plane lattice structure with the spacing of 0.208 nm, which corresponded to the (102) lattice of graphene. The topographic morphology of the NH2-GOQDs was observed by AFM (Fig. S5), and the thickness of NH2-GOQDs is about 5 nm, which reveals that most of NH2-GOQDs have four to six graphene layers [33,36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and chemical properties of FO membranes

ce:section.ce:para: The membrane surface morphology is observed by SEM. As shown in Fig. 3 , traditional TFC membrane shows a wrinkled surface with a ridge-valley structure, the typical morphology of PA layer generated by diffused MPD into the organic phase. Obviously, the surface morphology of other membranes containing NH2-GOQDs is different from that of TFC membrane. When NH2-GOQDs were embedded in PA layer, the membrane surface presented a ‘‘leaf-like’’ structure. And with the increase of NH2-GOQDs, all these novel TFN membranes seems to have broader ‘‘leaf-like’’ structure i.e. the leaves of TFC membrane are erected while TFN membrane are flat. It can be seen from Fig. S6 that the vertical PA sticks became smaller and the height of ridge in PA layer decreased from 430 nm to 245 nm. The transition of morphology suggested that the surface of the membrane became smooth, and the quantitative description was carried out by AFM which are shown in Fig. 4 and Table 2 . Clearly, the TFN membrane have a smaller surface roughness than TFC membrane, and the TFN-2 have the minimum Ra value of 29.2 nm. The transformation of surface roughness is consistent with SEM image. This phenomenon can be explained by the following reasons. During the polymerization reaction, MPD molecules diffused rapidly and reacted with TMC at the two-phase interface to form oligomers [27]. Then the amino groups on NH2-GOQDs cross-linked with the acid chloride groups on the oligomers and the carboxyl groups in the linear part of the polyamide to make them interact with each other. Finally, a compact PA nanonet was formed. This reaction would also accelerate the release of heat from the interface process, forming a smoother membrane surface [32]. In addition, it was also attributed to the steric hindrance effect of NH2-GOQDs nanoparticles. The existence of NH2-GOQDs blocked the diffusion of MPD to organic phase and delayed the formation of ridge in PA layer. It has been reported that the speed of the IP reaction affects the surface roughness of the TFC membrane [29]. Generally, the slow reaction rate will result in a smoother membrane surface. Whereas, when the concentration of nanoparticles increased to 50 ppm, NH2-GOQDs might be embedded in the pores of the PES substrate due to its quantum size [37]. Further increasing the concentration of NH2-GOQDs, obvious bead-like, irregular linear and massive agglomeration can be found on the membrane surface. The dense PA network structure can effectively inhibit the reverse diffusion of the solute in the draw solution. Moreover, the smooth surface will enhance the anti-fouling property of the membrane.

As we all know, the crystallinity of the membrane surface affects the molecular transport properties. Compared with crystal membrane, amorphous membrane have higher water permeability performance because of the broad void space [38]. As shown in Fig. S7, all test specimens display a broad XRD peak which is a typical structure of amorphous. And as the content of NH2-GOQDs increased to 50 ppm, peaks shifted to a higher degree value of 2θ indicating the decrease of d-spacing between adjacent polymeric chains (network pore) and improved retention for smaller ions. This phenomenon was caused by the fact that when the amino group on NH2-GOQDs reacted with TMC, it acted as a binder between polymer molecular chains [39]. However, with the further increase of NH2-GOQDs, the d-spacing became larger which could be caused by the aggregation of nanoparticles and the mutual exclusion of too many oxygen-containing functional groups.

Remarkably, when NH2-GOQDs were introduced into MPD aqueous solution, the functional groups (i.e., hydroxyl and amino) in NH2-GOQDs could interact with TMC molecules to form new polyamide nanostructure in the process of IP reaction. The functional groups on the membrane surface were characterized by FTIR. As shown in Fig. 5 , different from the PES substrate, all of the FO membranes shows a wide absorption peak at 3341 cm−1 due to the hydroxyl groups generated by the IP reaction [16]. The absorption band of TFN-2 membrane is stronger and wider than that of TFC membrane at 3341 cm−1, which may be caused by the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups in NH2-GOQDs. In addition, peaks at 1658 cm−1, 1543 cm−1, and 1245 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO (amide I), the coupling of in-plane NH bending and CN stretching vibration (amide II), and the absorption of SO in PES [40]. And the slight increase of peak intensity should be attributed to the NH2 groups in NH2-GOQDs participating in the IP reaction to form new amide bonds. The rise of proportion in peak area (A1658 cm−1/A1245 cm−1) represents the compact PA structure [27]. The change of the intensity about absorption peak implied that NH2-GOQDs were successfully integrated into the PA layer during the IP reaction and promoted the formation of a compact cross-linked network.

In order to prove the change of cross-linking degree of PA layer, XPS spectra were used for analysis. As we all know that the change of O/N ratio can not well reflect the degree of IP reaction in PA layer due to the presence of oxygen and nitrogen in NH2-GOQDs. Therefore, the content of chemical bonds obtained by deconvolution of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s high-resolution XPS spectra on the surface of membranes was used to assess the cross-linking degree [41]. As shown in Fig. 6 , the C 1s XPS spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.6 eV (CC, CH), 285.7 eV (CN), 287.8 eV (CO). In general, the reaction of amino and acyl chloride will lead to the formation of CN bonds. And when NH2-GOQDs were embedded in the PA layer, TFN-2 exhibited lower CO content compared to the TFC membrane (6.59%), which was 5.59%. In contrast, the ratio of CN increased from 26.54% to 33.20%. The rise of CN/CO ratio indicated that more amino groups participated in the IP reaction, thereby improving the cross-linking degree of PA layer [26,42]. Subsequently, we analyzed the N 1s and O 1s XPS spectra. As can be seen from Table 3 , TFN-2 showed a similar ratio of NH2 and a half lower -N+H3 content than TFC. This phenomenon indicated that more amide structure was formed in PA layer, which led to the decrease of chemical bonds caused by the protonation of amino groups in NH2-GOQDs. The results also showed that the addition of NH2-GOQDs could improve the cross-linking network structure of PA layer. In addition, when NH2-GOQDs were added to the PA layer, the proportion of OCO chemical bonds generated by the hydrolysis of unreacted acyl chloride groups [43] decreased gradually. The elevated amide functional groups proved that the cross-linking reaction on the membrane surface was adequate. Taken together, all of these data suggested the increase in the cross-linking degree of the PA layer. Combined with the surface morphology of membranes, the dense membrane structure is more likely to block the reverse osmosis of solute.

The surface charge property of the membrane has a certain extent influence on the rejection and adsorption of charged species in the solution [44,45]. Hence, the surface zeta potential of all membranes was investigated within a certain pH range of 2.0–9.0, and the test results are presented in Fig. 7 a. Generally speaking, the charge property of the membrane surface is related to the residual functional groups. As shown in Fig. 7a, all membranes had the similar change with the increasing of pH in solution, and the surface exhibited electronegativity in neutral solution. The reason was that acyl chloride groups on TMC which were not involved in the IP reaction would hydrolyze to produce carboxyl groups. Deprotonation of the carboxyl groups caused a negative charge on the membrane surface [46,47]. However, with the insertion of NH2-GOQDs, there was a slight transition towards positive charge on the PA layer surface. The results showed that NH2-GOQDs engaged in interfacial polymerization and consumed acyl chloride groups in TMC. It has been proved that the close proximity of the membrane surface to the electric neutral is beneficial to the improvement of anti-fouling property [48,49]. However, the nanoparticles were clustered together on the membrane surface when NH2-GOQDs content reached 400 ppm which caused the membrane surface shifted negatively slightly due to the formation of defects.

Furthermore, static water contact angle test was conducted to analyze the influence of nanoparticles on the hydrophilicity of the membrane surface. A lower value of contact angle represents more hydrophilic surface [50]. As shown in Fig. 7b, the contact angle of all modified membranes was lower than that of TFC membrane (75.9°) due to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups on NH2-GOQDs. When the NH2-GOQDs concentration was 50 ppm, the value reached the minimum of 42.1°. Whereas, as the content of NH2-GOQDs was further increased, the hydrophilicity of membrane surface decreased slightly. This was mainly because the agglomeration of NH2-GOQDs caused its uneven distribution on the membrane surface and a typical polyamide structure was exposed on the surface of the membrane. When the concentration of NH2-GOQDs was 400 ppm, it agglomerated into a lump on the membrane surface, causing a slight decrease of the contact angle. Overall, NH2-GOQDs as additive improved the hydrophilicity of PA layer and increased its affinity to water molecules. This will have a positive impact on the improvement of water flux [51–53].

The intrinsic transmission properties of fabricated membranes were investigated by the RO filtration setup. A and B values represent the permeability coefficient of pure water and salt (NaCl), respectively. As can be seen in Table 4 , all modified membranes exhibited a higher pure water permeability than that of traditional TFC membrane. The increase of A value might be attributed to the small-scale ridge structure and improved hydrophilicity of the PA layer, which reduced the water transmission distance and increased the attraction of the membrane to water molecules. It is worth noting that when NH2-GOQDs concentration was 50 and 100 ppm, the salt permeability was 2.42 ± 0.12 and 2.39 ± 0.12 × 10−8 m/s, respectively, lower than 2.82 ± 0.23 × 10−8 m/s of TFC membrane, which proved that the traditional trade-off effect was broken. Furthermore, the B/A ratio has been considered as a criterion for assessing membrane selectivity. A smaller B/A ratio indicates a better permselectivity. After NH2-GOQDs incorporation, the modified membrane (with the exception of TFN-5) exhibited about 30% lower B/A ratio compared with the TFC membrane. And the value of TFN-2 reached the minimum of 5.16 ± 0.51 kPa, which was expected to have a superior FO performance.

In order to maintain the balance of solution concentration on both sides of the membrane, solute in the draw solution will reverse permeate into the feed solution. Generally speaking, the high reverse salt flux indicates that the solute leaks into the feed solution seriously, and then leads to serious ICP and membrane surface pollution. Therefore, the ideal FO membrane should show high water flux and low reverse salt flux. From the value of Js in Fig. 8b, the TFC membrane exhibited high reverse salt flux of 8.30 gMH (FO mode) and 11.7 gMH (PRO mode), respectively. All the TFN membranes displayed lower reverse salt flux compared with that of TFC membrane. The values of TFN-2, TFN-3 and TFN-5 were 6.0, 6.40 and 5.48 gMH under PRO mode, respectively, which decreased by about 50%. When NH2-GOQDs were added to the IP process, NH2-GOQDs reacted with acyl chloride groups on the oligomers and carboxyl groups in the linear part of polyamide molecule. This cross-linking reaction connected the gap between oligomers and improved the cross-linking degree of PA layer, thus forming a dense network structure, which effectively resisted reverse osmosis of solutes. In addition, NH2-GOQDs might be anchored in substrate [37] resulting in a smaller hole channel where the diffusion of salt molecules was blocked. The diffusion diagram of reactants in the IP process is shown in Fig. 10
. For TFN-4, the reverse salt flux was slightly increased, which was mainly induced by the localized defects caused by the uneven distribution of NH2-GOQDs on the membrane surface.

The ratio of reverse salt flux to water flux (Js /Jv ) can be considered as a comprehensive index to evaluate the FO performance (Fig. 9). Generally speaking, the membrane with lower value of Js /Jv implies better permselectivity to water. In this study, the ratio of Js /Jv exhibits a very obvious decline after NH2-GOQDs incorporation, then shows a trend of first decreasing and then increasing with the rise of concentration. TFN-2 membrane reached the minimum of 0.20 g/L compared with other membranes including the traditional TFC membrane (0.49 g/L) under FO mode. The result is consistent with the value of B/A which indicates that incorporation of NH2-GOQDs into the PA layer with appropriate concentration can significantly improve productivity and selectivity, and the schematic diagram of water permeation and salt retention process is shown in Fig. 11 . In addition, the FO performances of TFN membranes as well as the other reported and commercial membranes are presented in Table 5 . Compared with these membranes, the TFN-2 and TFN-3 membrane in this study still show excellent desalination performance.

Generally speaking, the addition of nanoparticle in the PA layer will lead to the weakening of the connection between the molecular chains in the polymer film and the interfacial adhesion between the PA layer and the substrate. Therefore, in order to estimate the filtration stability of the membrane, a long-term FO test was performed. As shown in Fig. 12 a, the water flux of TFC and TFN-2 membrane dropped significantly after 12 h of operation. This phenomenon could be explained by the decrease of the osmotic pressure difference between the two sides of the membrane due to the dilution of the draw solution and the inspissation of the feed solution, thus reducing the driving force on water. It was worth noting that TFN-2 membrane had higher water flux due to its more hydrophilic surface and lower water transport distance. However, this phenomenon led to more serious dilution of the draw solution. Therefore, the drop of osmotic pressure difference was more obvious with the increase of time, and correspondingly, the water flux of TFN-2 decreased more significantly. During the long-term FO test, the conductivity value of feed solution was recorded every two hours to analyze the corresponding reverse solute flux. Fig. 12b shows the ratio of reverse solute flux to water flux (Js /Jv ). It can be seen from Fig. 12b that TFC membrane shows a trend of decreasing at first and then increasing. The reason for the increase of Js /Jv ratio was that the active layer partially dropped off by water flushing in the cross-flow filtration process, resulting in its poor selectivity. However, when the NH2-GOQDs were cross-linked in the polyamide chain, the TFN-2 membrane showed stable selectivity during a long-term FO test.

Furthermore, the adhesion between the substrate and the PA layer was probed and observed quantitatively by an in-situ nano-indentation/scratch technique and the corresponding results are shown in Fig. 12 and Table 6 . Similar reports [62–65] have proved the feasibility of this technology. According to the time corresponding to the force jump point (critical point), the normal phase force which reflects the magnitude of interface adhesion force can be found accordingly. It can be seen from Fig. 12c, Fig. 12d and Table 6 that interface adhesion force increases with the embedding of NH2-GOQDs. This was attributed to the irreversible shrinkage of the substrate during the heat treatment (as shown in Fig. S8), so that NH2-GOQDs were physically anchored in the substrate. The reaction between NH2-GOQDs and TMC promoted the connection between the substrate and PA layer, which led to the increase of adhesion. The improved interfacial adhesion was the main reason for the long-term filtration stability of TFN membrane.

Herein, NH2-GOQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal reaction and chemically cross-linked in the PA active layer via IP reaction. This reinforced polyamide nanostructure endowed the PA layer with a small-scale ridge structure (i.e. flat “leaf-like” morphology), compact and smooth membrane surface observed by FIB-SEM, SEM and AFM images. The change of molecular chain spacing in the crystal structure and zeta potential proved the successful incorporation of NH2-GOQDs. Remarkably, the introduction of amino groups and oxygen-containing functional groups greatly increased the hydrophilicity of the membrane surface, and the water contact angle was decreased from 75.1 to 42.1°(a reduction of approximately 45%). FTIR and XPS measurement confirmed that NH2-GOQDs participated in the IP process and improved the cross-linking reaction, resulting in a dense PA layer. Owning to the small water transmission distance, excellent hydrophilicity, and dense polyamide structure, when we evaluated the membrane intrinsic transport properties under pressure-driven, the novel TFN membrane exhibited an improved pure water flux with A value of 4.69 ± 0.17 × 10−12 m⋅s−1⋅Pa−1 compared to that of the TFC membrane (3.78 ± 0.31 × 10−12 m⋅s−1⋅Pa−1) while holding a higher NaCl rejection of over 97%. In the PRO mode of FO testing, the TFN-2 membrane (50 ppm NH2-GOQDs) correspondingly exhibited a 21% higher water flux and a 50% lower reverse salt flux. And the value of Js /Jv reached the minimum value. Furthermore, the TFN-2 membrane showed stable selectivity in the long-term FO test. In order to investigate the reason for the improvement of stability, the nano-indentation/scratch technique was conducted to analyze quantitatively the adhesion between the substrate and the PA layer, and the results showed that TFN membrane had a stronger interface adhesion due to the physical anchoring of NH2-GOQDs in the substrate. In general, NH2-GOQDs as reactive additive to modify PA layer effectively improves the desalination performance and stability in the FO process, and the well-designed TFN membrane will also be expected to have good anti-fouling property due to the smooth membrane surface.
